g.data-narration: Welcome to Sequential Circuits. Today we explore how digital systems remember past events and make decisions based on history.
g.text: Sequential Circuits - The Memory of Digital Systems

g.data-narration: Unlike combinational circuits with no memory, sequential circuits remember past inputs, enabling complex digital behavior like counting.
A sequential circuit has two main parts: combinational logic for processing and flip-flops for storing the present state.
Flip-flops are bistable memory devices that store one bit. They are the fundamental building blocks of all sequential circuits.
g.text: A flip-flop stores 1 bit (0 or 1) and changes state based on clock and inputs

g.data-narration: The SR flip-flop is the simplest type. S sets Q to 1, R resets Q to 0. But S=R=1 is forbidden—it creates invalid state.
The JK flip-flop solves the SR invalid state problem. When J=K=1, it toggles—Q becomes NOT Q, enabling counting behavior.
The D flip-flop is the most widely used. The output Q simply copies the D input at each clock pulse—simple and reliable.
g.text: Output equals input on clock edge
✓ Most popular • ✓ Edge-triggered • ✓ No invalid states

g.data-narration: The T flip-flop has one input T. When T=1, it toggles on each clock pulse. When T=0, it holds its current state.
g.text: Perfect for: Counters and frequency dividers

g.data-narration: Synchronous sequential circuits use a clock—a square wave that triggers state changes at rising or falling edges, ensuring orderly operation.
g.text: State changes at clock edges • Predictable • Most common

g.data-narration: In Moore machines, output depends only on state. In Mealy machines, output depends on both state and inputs—more flexible but complex.
g.text: Outputs can change between clock pulses

g.data-narration: A 2-bit counter cycles through states 00→01→10→11→00. T0 always toggles, T1 toggles when Q0=1—simple but powerful!
This timing diagram shows how the counter works: Clock triggers, Q0 toggles every cycle, Q1 toggles when Q0 was 1 previously.
g.text: Timing Diagram: How the Counter Operates
